retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The hypothalamus functions in conjunction with the pituitary gland through the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. The hypothalamus itself contains several types of neurons that release different hormones. The thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH), corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), somatostatin, and dopamine are released from the hypothalamus into the blood and travel to the anterior pituitary.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis is a blood portal system connecting the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary, allowing control of several hormones. The hypothalamus is connected anatomically to the pituitary gland via the infundibulum. Within the infundibulum are capillaries that pour into portal veins, flowing directly to the anterior pituitary. This system ensures that the hormones released from the hypothalamus circulate directly into the anterior pituitary gland, never entering the general circulation. The hormones released from the hypothalamus include corticotropin-releasing hormone, thyrotropin-releasing hormone, gonadotropin-releasing hormone, growth hormone-releasing hormone, somatostatin, prolactin-releasing hormone, and dopamine. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The thyrotropin-releasing hormone is a tripeptide that stimulates the release of thyroid-stimulating hormone and prolactin from the anterior pituitary gland. The gonadotropin-releasing hormone triggers sexual development at the onset of puberty and maintains female and male physiology after that by controlling the release of follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone. The growth hormone-releasing hormone stimulates the secretion of growth hormone by the anterior pituitary. The corticotropin-releasing hormone stimulates the release of adrenocorticotropic hormone from the anterior pituitary. Somatostatin inhibits the release of both growth hormone and thyroid-stimulating hormone, and various intestinal hormones. Dopamine inhibits the release of prolactin from the anterior pituitary, modulates motor-control centers, and activates the reward centers of the brain. Prolactin functions mainly to promote lactation but also helps regulate reproduction, metabolism, and the immune system.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Central Nervous System -- Structure and Function -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: While the hypothalamus is one of the smallest parts of the brain, it is vital to maintaining homeostasis. The hypothalamus connects the CNS to the endocrine system. It is responsible for heart rate, blood pressure, appetite, thirst, temperature, and the release of various hormones. The hypothalamus also communicates with the pituitary gland to release or inhibit antidiuretic hormone, corticotropin-releasing hormone, gonadotropin-releasing hormone, growth hormone-releasing hormone, prolactin-inhibiting hormone, thyroid-releasing hormone, oxytocin. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Appetite And Weight Regulation -- Organ Systems Involved -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: The arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus is located adjacent to the median eminence and 3rd ventricle. The area is highly vascularized. Thus, the arcuate nucleus is exposed to hormones passing through the blood-brain barrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The hypothalamus is the region in the ventral brain which coordinates the endocrine system. It receives many signals from various regions of the brain and in return, releases both releasing and inhibiting hormones, which then act on the pituitary gland to direct the functions of the thyroid gland, adrenal glands, and reproductive organs and to influence growth, fluid balance, and milk production. [1] It is also involved in the non-endocrine functions of temperature regulation, regulation of the autonomic nervous system, and the control of appetite.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Organ Systems Involved
retrieved_docs.content: GnRH is a central regulator of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis. The neurons that produce GnRH are in the hypothalamus, specifically in the infundibular nucleus. Once secreted, GnRH acts on the anterior pituitary where follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) are secreted and modulate sex steroid production from the gonads. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: Corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) is another hormone in the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. CRH is produced in the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus (PVH), which is released and stimulates the anterior pituitary gland to release adrenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH). [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Hypothalamus -- Structure and Function -- Arcuate Nucleus
retrieved_docs.content: The arcuate nucleus releases hormones secreted by axon terminals into the hypothalamohypophysial venous portal system to control anterior pituitary hormone release. The corticotropin-releasing hormone causes the anterior pituicytes to release adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) into capillaries, which drain into the cerebral venous system. ACTH travels through systemic circulation to stimulate the adrenal cortex (zona reticularis) production of the stress-response hormone cortisol. Given its diversity of inputs, the hypothalamus enables the body to respond to physiological and psychological stressors. Again, cortisol production varies throughout a 24-hour day, with the highest level around sunrise and the lowest around sunset due to the interconnections between the arcuate and suprachiasmatic nuclei. Thyrotropin-releasing hormone leads to systemic TSH secretion, increasing the synthesis and release of thyroid hormone to regulate metabolism. The hypothalamus senses body energy stores in part by receptors for the adipocyte hormone leptin. When reserves are low, the hypothalamus decreases metabolism by decreasing the thyroid hormone.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) is composed of 3 peptides, pyroglutamyl-histidyl-prolineamide. TRH begins as pro-TRH and, through a process of peptization and cyclization of glutamine, forms a pyroglutamyl residue. [10] TRH metabolizes rapidly, with a plasma half-life of approximately 3 minutes. TRH is another hormone involved in the HPA axis, helping determine the regulation of TSH secretion. Specifically, TRH is found in the highest concentrations in the PVH and median eminence of the hypothalamus. However, it is also present in the central nervous system, gastrointestinal tract, pancreatic islets, pituitary gland, and reproductive tracts – TRH function at these sites is unknown. Lastly, either high levels or exogenous administration of TRH stimulates additional hormones besides TSH, especially prolactin. [28] [29] [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior pituitary is an extension of axonal projections from the supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei of the hypothalamus nervous tissue, where oxytocin and anti-diuretic hormones are stored in the axonal ends or Herring bodies. [1] Oxytocin secretion results in a positive feedback loop during childbirth, increasing contractions. Oxytocin also affects lactation. Oxytocin is discussed in detail in this article. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Anterior Pituitary -- Organ Systems Involved -- Hypothalamic-Pituitary-GH Axis
retrieved_docs.content: Growth hormone production from the anterior pituitary is regulated by the stimulatory and inhibitory control of the hypothalamus. Hypothalamus produces growth hormone-releasing hormone that stimulates the somatotrophs of the anterior pituitary to secrete growth hormone. Somatostatin produced by the hypothalamus inhibits the secretion of growth hormone from the anterior pituitary. Once released into the circulation, growth hormone increases the expression of the insulin-like growth factor 1 ( IGF-1 ) gene to a greater extent in the liver compared to other tissues. IGF-1 mediates the effects of GH on growth etc

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Anterior Pituitary -- Organ Systems Involved -- Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis
retrieved_docs.content: The corticotrophin-releasing hormone (CRH) of the hypothalamus stimulates the corticotrophs in the anterior pituitary to secrete corticotrophin or ACTH, the thyrotropin-releasing hormone stimulates the thyrotrophs to secrete TSH, growth hormone-releasing hormone stimulates the somatotrophs to secrete growth hormone (GH), luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone stimulates gonadotrophs to secrete FSH and LH. Unlike the other releasing hormones that induce secretion of anterior pituitary hormones, dopamine inhibits the production of prolactin by lactotrophs. Likewise, somatostatin inhibits the production of GH.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Organ Systems Involved
retrieved_docs.content: The hypothalamus ultimately affects the functions of the pituitary gland, thyroid gland, adrenal glands, kidneys, musculoskeletal system, and reproductive organs.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Hormones -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The hormones of the pituitary gland are protein or polypeptide in nature and vary in complexity.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Hormones -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The pituitary hormones are special chemical messengers that are produced by the pituitary gland, also known as "the master gland of the body." The hormones are peptides or glycoproteins in nature and play a vital role in regulating the functions of other endocrine glands. The anterior pituitary hormones are produced by five different endocrine cell types (somatotropes, gonadotropes, lactotrophs, corticotropes, and thyrotropes) and include growth hormone (GH), prolactin, adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and luteinizing hormone (LH), adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH). The release of these hormones is under the regulation of inhibitory or stimulatory signals from the hypothalamus. The posterior pituitary hormones are the nano peptide and oligo-peptide vasopressin and oxytocin, respectively, which regulate water retention and uterine contraction. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1303
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES is defined as an overarching class of endocrine agents that includes both anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues as well as hypothalamic hormones (hypotalamické hormony). In this hierarchy, anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues are considered a subset of the broader PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES group, reflecting the close functional relationship between pituitary secretions and their regulatory hypothalamic counterparts. At the same time, the entire PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES category is regarded as a type of hypothalamic hormone group, underscoring the integrated nature of pituitary‑hypothalamic endocrine pathways and the inclusion of synthetic analogues that mimic these natural hormones. This comprehensive classification aids in understanding the shared physiology, therapeutic targeting, and clinical management of disorders involving pituitary and hypothalamic hormone systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), is classified as a member of the peptidy group, meaning it is a peptide hormone that functions within the body’s endocrine signaling pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 621
retrieved_docs.content: ACTH and synthetic analog preparations are classified as anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues, and cosyntropin, a synthetic ACTH analog, is a member of this group. The broader class PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES includes anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues and is itself a subclass of hypotalamické hormony (hypothalamic hormones). This hierarchy places ACTH and its synthetic analogs within the larger framework of pituitary and hypothalamic hormonal systems, linking them to both anterior pituitary hormone activity and the broader category of hypothalamic hormones.

Collectively, these relationships underscore the dual roles of many entities—e.g., Corynebacterium diphtheriae and ACTH—in immune regulation and metabolism, the hierarchical placement of hormones within endocrine axes, and the importance of integrating immunologic, metabolic and pharmacologic information for comprehensive patient assessment and therapeutic planning.

Together, these relationships illustrate how diverse pharmaceutical formulations can combine multiple hormone‑based actives, how ACTH fits within the endocrine hierarchy linking pituitary hormones to uterotonic activity, and how peptide hormones such as ACTH, erythropoietin, and glucagon are unified by their proteinaceous nature while serving different regulatory functions in the body. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the multifaceted actions of hormone‑containing products and their potential systemic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: the relationships illustrate how anterior pituitary hormones and their synthetic analogues are organized within the larger pituitary‑hypothalamic hormonal framework, how therapeutic groups such as somatropin agonists and thyrotropin agents are defined, and how a diverse array of diagnostic agents—including hormone analogues, microbial preparations, and imaging compounds—are integral to assessing and managing endocrine function and related pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1893
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and its analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2003
retrieved_docs.content: Hypothalamic hormones include a subgroup referred to as hormony inhibující uvolňování hypofyzárního hormonu, which function to inhibit the release of pituitary hormones and thereby regulate endocrine activity.

This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the pharmacological content of complex formulations and the physiological contexts in which the constituent hormones operate.

Overall, the knowledge base links specific thyroid‑acting drugs, their classification, and their physiological targets with diagnostic and therapeutic procedures that focus on the endocrine system, while highlighting the hierarchical and functional relationships among thyroid preparations, thyroid hormones, the thyroid gland, pancreatic hormones, and broader endocrine functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1417
retrieved_docs.content: ACTH and synthetic analog preparations are classified as anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues, indicating that they belong to the group of hormones produced by the anterior pituitary gland as well as their synthetic counterparts. Cosyntropin is identified as a member of the ACTH and synthetic analog preparations group, representing a specific synthetic ACTH analogue used in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 708
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is recognized as a specific hormone, indicating its role as a biologically active steroid that participates in endocrine signaling. Endocannabinoids are linked to the hormone category, suggesting that they either belong to a subclass of hormones or possess hormone‑like actions within the body. This relationship underscores the interconnected nature of steroid hormones such as estradiol and the endocannabinoid system, both of which contribute to the regulation of physiological processes through hormonal mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 551
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader class of hypothalamic and pituitary hormones. Within hierarchical classifications, hypothalamic and pituitary hormones are considered a subclass of uterotonics—agents that stimulate uterine contraction. Uterotonics themselves are linked to vasotocin, a peptide hormone closely related to oxytocin that also exerts uterine‑contractile effects. From a biochemical perspective, ACTH, erythropoietin, and glucagon are all members of the “amino acid, peptide, or protein” family, reflecting their nature as peptide hormones derived from amino‑acid chains. This inverse‑ISA relationship underscores that these hormones share fundamental structural characteristics despite their distinct physiological roles: ACTH stimulates adrenal cortisol production, erythropoietin regulates red‑blood‑cell formation, and glucagon raises blood glucose levels. Together, these relationships illustrate how diverse pharmaceutical formulations can combine multiple hormone‑based actives, how ACTH fits
